Pursuant to the [Twentieth Amendment](/wiki/Twentieth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twentieth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), the four-year term of office for both the president and the vice president begins at noon on January 20, in the year following the preceding presidential election.[[147]](#cite_note-153) The first presidential and vice presidential terms to begin on this date, known as [Inauguration Day](/wiki/United_States_presidential_inauguration "United States presidential inauguration"), were the [second terms](/wiki/Second_inauguration_of_Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Second inauguration of Franklin D. Roosevelt") of President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") and Vice President [John Nance Garner](/wiki/John_Nance_Garner "John Nance Garner") in 1937.[[148]](#cite_note-HHistory1201937-154) Previously, Inauguration Day was on March 4. As a result of the date change, the first term (1933–37) of both men had been shortened by 43 days.[[149]](#cite_note-GPOCONAN20171021-155)

Before executing the powers of the office, a president is required to [recite](/wiki/Recitation "Recitation") the [presidential Oath of Office](/wiki/Oath_of_office_of_the_president_of_the_United_States "Oath of office of the president of the United States"), found in [Article II, Section 1, Clause 8 of the Constitution](/wiki/Article_Two_of_the_United_States_Constitution#Clause_8:_Oath_or_affirmation "Article Two of the United States Constitution"). This is the only component in the inauguration ceremony mandated by the Constitution:

> I do solemnly swear (or [affirm](/wiki/Affirmation_in_law "Affirmation in law")) that I will faithfully execute the Office of President of the United States, and will to the best of my ability, preserve, protect, and defend the Constitution of the United States.[[150]](#cite_note-VK218-156)

Presidents have traditionally placed one hand upon a [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") while taking the oath, and have added "So help me God" to the end of the oath.[[151]](#cite_note-157)[[152]](#cite_note-158) Although the oath may be administered by any person authorized by law to administer oaths, presidents are traditionally sworn in by the [chief justice of the United States](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States").[[150]](#cite_note-VK218-156)

## Incumbency

### Term limit

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/William_MacKinley_%281843-1901%29%2C_de_25ste_president_van_de_Verenigde_Staten_van_Amerika%2C_met_naast%2C_SFA002006258.jpg/250px-William_MacKinley_%281843-1901%29%2C_de_25ste_president_van_de_Verenigde_Staten_van_Amerika%2C_met_naast%2C_SFA002006258.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_MacKinley_(1843-1901),_de_25ste_president_van_de_Verenigde_Staten_van_Amerika,_met_naast,_SFA002006258.jpg)

President [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley") and his vice presidential running mate, New York governor [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), c. 1900

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/FDR_1944_Color_Portrait.jpg/250px-FDR_1944_Color_Portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:FDR_1944_Color_Portrait.jpg)

[Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") won a record four [presidential elections](/wiki/United_States_presidential_election "United States presidential election") in [1932](/wiki/1932_United_States_presidential_election "1932 United States presidential election"), [1936](/wiki/1936_United_States_presidential_election "1936 United States presidential election"), [1940](/wiki/1940_United_States_presidential_election "1940 United States presidential election"), and [1944](/wiki/1944_United_States_presidential_election "1944 United States presidential election") prior to the implementation of the [22nd amendment](/wiki/Twenty-second_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-second Amendment to the United States Constitution") in 1951, which instituted a two-term limit.

When the first president, [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington"), announced in his [Farewell Address](/wiki/George_Washington%27s_Farewell_Address "George Washington's Farewell Address") that he was not running for a third term, he established a "two terms then out" precedent. Precedent became tradition after [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") publicly embraced the principle a decade later during his second term, as did his two immediate successors, [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison") and [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe "James Monroe").[[153]](#cite_note-TermsTenure-159) In spite of the strong two-term tradition, [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") sought nomination at the [1880 Republican National Convention](/wiki/1880_Republican_National_Convention "1880 Republican National Convention") for a non-consecutive third term, but was unsuccessful.[[154]](#cite_note-160)

In 1940, after leading the nation through the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and focused on supporting U.S. [allied nations](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") at war with the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers"), Franklin Roosevelt was elected to a third term, breaking the long-standing precedent. Four years later, with the U.S. engaged in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), he was re-elected again despite his declining physical health; he died 82 days into his fourth term on April 12, 1945.[[155]](#cite_note-22ndAPPC-161)

In response to the unprecedented length of Roosevelt's presidency, the [Twenty-second Amendment](/wiki/Twenty-second_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-second Amendment to the United States Constitution") was [adopted](/wiki/Ratification "Ratification") in 1951. The amendment bars anyone from being elected president more than twice, or once if that person served more than two years (24 months) of another president's four-year term. [Harry S. Truman](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman "Harry S. Truman"), the president at the time it was submitted to the states by the Congress, was exempted from its limitations. Without the exemption, he would not have been eligible to run for a second full term in 1952, which he briefly sought, as he had served nearly all of Franklin Roosevelt's unexpired 1945–1949 term and had been elected to a full four-year term beginning in 1949.[[155]](#cite_note-22ndAPPC-161)

### Vacancies and succession

Main article: [United States presidential line of succession](/wiki/United_States_presidential_line_of_succession "United States presidential line of succession")

Under [Section 1 of the Twenty-fifth Amendment](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution#Section_1:_Presidential_succession "Twenty-fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), ratified in 1967, the vice president becomes president upon the [removal from office](/wiki/Federal_impeachment_trial_in_the_United_States "Federal impeachment trial in the United States"), death, or resignation of the president. Deaths have occurred a number of times, resignation has occurred only once, and removal from office has never occurred.

Before the ratification of the Twenty-fifth amendment (which clarified the matter of succession), [Article II, Section 1, Clause 6](/wiki/Article_Two_of_the_United_States_Constitution#Clause_6:_Vacancy_and_disability "Article Two of the United States Constitution"), stated only that the vice president assumes the "powers and duties" of the presidency in the event of a president's removal, death, resignation, or inability.[[156]](#cite_note-FordhamLaw2011-162) Under this clause, there was ambiguity about whether the vice president would actually become president in the event of a vacancy, or simply [act](/wiki/Acting_(law) "Acting (law)") as president,[[157]](#cite_note-ArticleIIessays-163) potentially resulting in a [special election](/wiki/Special_election "Special election"). Upon the death of President [William Henry Harrison](/wiki/William_Henry_Harrison "William Henry Harrison") in 1841, Vice President [John Tyler](/wiki/John_Tyler "John Tyler") declared that he had succeeded to the office itself, refusing to accept any papers addressed to the "Acting President", and Congress ultimately accepted it.

In the event of a double vacancy, Article II, Section 1, Clause 6 also authorizes Congress to declare who shall become acting president in the "Case of Removal, Death, Resignation or Inability, both of the president and vice president".[[157]](#cite_note-ArticleIIessays-163) The [Presidential Succession Act](/wiki/Presidential_Succession_Act "Presidential Succession Act") of 1947 (codified as 3 U.S.C. § 19) provides that if both the president and vice president have left office or are both otherwise unavailable to serve during their terms of office, the [presidential line of succession](/wiki/United_States_presidential_line_of_succession "United States presidential line of succession") follows the order of: speaker of the House, then, if necessary, the president pro tempore of the Senate, and then if necessary, the eligible heads of [federal executive departments](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments") who form the president's [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_States "Cabinet of the United States").[[158]](#cite_note-cnn.com-164)

The cabinet currently has 15 members, of which the secretary of state is first in line. The other Cabinet secretaries follow in the order in which their department, or the department of which their department is the successor, was created. Those individuals who are constitutionally ineligible to be elected to the presidency are also disqualified from assuming the powers and duties of the presidency through succession. No statutory successor has yet been called upon to act as president.[[158]](#cite_note-cnn.com-164)

### Declarations of inability

Main article: [Twenty-fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution")

Under the Twenty-fifth Amendment, the president may temporarily transfer the presidential powers and duties to the vice president, who then becomes [acting president](/wiki/Acting_president_of_the_United_States "Acting president of the United States"), by transmitting to the [speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") and the [president *pro tempore* of the Senate](/wiki/President_pro_tempore_of_the_United_States_Senate "President pro tempore of the United States Senate") a statement that he is unable to discharge his duties. The president resumes his or her powers upon transmitting a second declaration stating that he is again able. The mechanism has been used by [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") (once), [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") (twice), and [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") (once), each in anticipation of surgery.[[159]](#cite_note-165)[[160]](#cite_note-166)

The Twenty-fifth Amendment also provides that the vice president, together with a majority of certain members of the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_States "Cabinet of the United States"), may transfer the presidential powers and duties to the vice president by transmitting a written declaration, to the speaker of the House and the president *pro tempore* of the Senate, to the effect that the president is unable to discharge his or her powers and duties. If the president then declares that no such inability exist, he or she resumes the presidential powers unless the vice president and Cabinet make a second declaration of presidential inability, in which case Congress decides the question.

### Removal

Main articles: [List of efforts to impeach presidents of the United States](/wiki/List_of_efforts_to_impeach_presidents_of_the_United_States "List of efforts to impeach presidents of the United States"), [Federal impeachment in the United States](/wiki/Federal_impeachment_in_the_United_States "Federal impeachment in the United States"), and [Federal impeachment trial in the United States](/wiki/Federal_impeachment_trial_in_the_United_States "Federal impeachment trial in the United States")

[Article II, Section 4](/wiki/Article_Two_of_the_United_States_Constitution#Section_4:_Impeachment "Article Two of the United States Constitution") of the Constitution allows for the removal of high federal officials, including the president, from office for "[treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason"), [bribery](/wiki/Bribery "Bribery"), or other [high crimes and misdemeanors](/wiki/High_crimes_and_misdemeanors "High crimes and misdemeanors")". [Article I, Section 2, Clause 5](/wiki/Article_One_of_the_United_States_Constitution#Clause_5:_Speaker_and_other_officers;_Impeachment "Article One of the United States Constitution") authorizes the House of Representatives to serve as a "[grand jury](/wiki/Grand_jury "Grand jury")" with the power to impeach said officials by a majority vote.[[161]](#cite_note-A1essaySBP-167) [Article I, Section 3, Clause 6](/wiki/Article_One_of_the_United_States_Constitution#Clause_6:_Trial_of_Impeachments "Article One of the United States Constitution") authorizes the Senate to serve as a [court](/wiki/Court "Court") with the power to remove impeached officials from office, by a two-thirds vote to convict.[[162]](#cite_note-168)

Three presidents have been impeached by the House of Representatives: Andrew Johnson in [1868](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Andrew_Johnson "Impeachment of Andrew Johnson"), Bill Clinton in [1998](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Bill_Clinton "Impeachment of Bill Clinton"), and Donald Trump in [2019](/wiki/First_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "First impeachment of Donald Trump") and [2021](/wiki/Second_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "Second impeachment of Donald Trump"). None have been convicted by the Senate. The [House Judiciary Committee](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_the_Judiciary "United States House Committee on the Judiciary") conducted an impeachment inquiry against Richard Nixon in [1973–74](/wiki/Impeachment_process_against_Richard_Nixon "Impeachment process against Richard Nixon") and reported three articles of impeachment to the House of Representatives for final action. He resigned from office before the House voted on them.[[161]](#cite_note-A1essaySBP-167)

### Circumvention of authority

Controversial measures have sometimes been taken short of removal to deal with perceived recklessness on the part of the president, or with a long-term disability. In some cases, staff have intentionally failed to deliver messages to or from the president, typically to avoid executing or promoting the president to write certain orders. This has ranged from [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon")'s Chief of Staff not transmitting orders to the Cabinet due to the president's heavy drinking, to staff removing memos from [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")'s desk.[[163]](#cite_note-169) Decades before the Twenty-fifth Amendment, in 1919, President Woodrow Wilson had a [stroke that left him partly incapacitated](/wiki/Presidency_of_Woodrow_Wilson#Incapacity,_1919–1921 "Presidency of Woodrow Wilson"). First lady [Edith Wilson](/wiki/Edith_Wilson "Edith Wilson") kept this condition a secret from the public for a while, and controversially [became the sole gatekeeper](/wiki/Edith_Wilson#Increased_role_after_husband's_stroke "Edith Wilson") for access to the president (aside from his doctor), assisting him with paperwork and deciding which information was "important" enough to share with him.

| 1873 | $50,000 | $1,343,750 |

Since 2001, the president's annual salary has been $400,000, along with a $50,000 expense allowance; a $100,000 nontaxable travel account; and a $19,000 entertainment[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] account. The president's salary is set by Congress, and under [Article II, Section 1, Clause 7](/wiki/Article_Two_of_the_United_States_Constitution#Clause_7:_Salary "Article Two of the United States Constitution") of the Constitution, any increase or reduction in presidential salary cannot take effect before the next presidential term of office.[[167]](#cite_note-173)[[10]](#cite_note-cnbc-10)

### Residence

For the official residences in which President Washington resided, see [Presidency of George Washington § Residences](/wiki/Presidency_of_George_Washington#Residences "Presidency of George Washington"). For the private residences of the various U.S. presidents, see [List of residences of presidents of the United States](/wiki/List_of_residences_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "List of residences of presidents of the United States").

The [Executive Residence](/wiki/Executive_Residence "Executive Residence") of the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), is the [official residence](/wiki/Official_residence "Official residence") of the president. The site was selected by George Washington, and the cornerstone was laid in 1792. Every president since John Adams in 1800 has lived there. At various times in U.S. history, it has been known as the "President's Palace", the "President's House", and the "Executive Mansion". Theodore Roosevelt officially gave the White House its current name in 1901.[[168]](#cite_note-174) The federal government pays for state dinners and other official functions, but the president pays for personal, family, and guest dry cleaning and food.[[169]](#cite_note-175)

[Camp David](/wiki/Camp_David "Camp David"), officially titled Naval Support Facility Thurmont, a mountain-based military camp in [Frederick County, Maryland](/wiki/Frederick_County,_Maryland "Frederick County, Maryland"), is the president's country residence. A place of solitude and tranquility, the site has been used extensively to host foreign dignitaries since the 1940s.[[170]](#cite_note-176)

The [President's Guest House](/wiki/President%27s_Guest_House "President's Guest House"), located next to the [Eisenhower Executive Office Building](/wiki/Eisenhower_Executive_Office_Building "Eisenhower Executive Office Building") at the White House Complex and [Lafayette Park](/wiki/Lafayette_Square,_Washington,_D.C. "Lafayette Square, Washington, D.C."), serves as the president's official guest house and as a secondary residence for the president if needed. Four interconnected, 19th-century houses—Blair House, Lee House, and 700 and 704 Jackson Place—with a combined floor space exceeding 70,000 square feet (6,500 m2) constitute the property.[[171]](#cite_note-177)

### Travel

Main article: [Transportation of the president of the United States](/wiki/Transportation_of_the_president_of_the_United_States "Transportation of the president of the United States")

The primary means of long-distance air travel for the president is one of two identical [Boeing VC-25](/wiki/Boeing_VC-25 "Boeing VC-25") aircraft, which are extensively modified [Boeing 747](/wiki/Boeing_747 "Boeing 747") airliners and are referred to as [Air Force One](/wiki/Air_Force_One "Air Force One") while the president is on board. Any U.S. Air Force aircraft the president is aboard is designated as "Air Force One" for the duration of the flight. In-country trips are typically handled with just one of the two planes, while overseas trips are handled with both, one primary and one backup. The president has access to smaller Air Force aircraft, most notably the [Boeing C-32](/wiki/Boeing_C-32 "Boeing C-32"), which are used when the president must travel to airports that cannot support a jumbo jet. Any civilian aircraft the president is aboard is designated [Executive One](/wiki/Executive_One "Executive One") for the flight.[[172]](#cite_note-af1-178)

For short-distance air travel, the president has access to a fleet of [U.S. Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") helicopters of varying models, designated [Marine One](/wiki/Marine_One "Marine One") when the president is aboard any particular one in the fleet. Flights are typically handled with as many as five helicopters all flying together and frequently swapping positions as to disguise which helicopter the president is actually aboard to any would-be threats.

For ground travel, the president uses the [presidential state car](/wiki/Presidential_state_car_(United_States) "Presidential state car (United States)"), which is an armored [limousine](/wiki/Limousine "Limousine") designed to look like a [Cadillac](/wiki/Cadillac "Cadillac") sedan, but built on a truck [chassis](/wiki/Chassis "Chassis").[[173]](#cite_note-USSSPRL-179)[[174]](#cite_note-CNN20090106-180) The [U.S. Secret Service](/wiki/United_States_Secret_Service "United States Secret Service") operates and maintains the fleet of several limousines. The president also has access to [two armored motorcoaches](/wiki/Ground_Force_One "Ground Force One"), which are primarily used for [touring trips](/wiki/Whistle_stop_train_tour "Whistle stop train tour").[[175]](#cite_note-181)

### Protection

Main article: [United States Secret Service](/wiki/United_States_Secret_Service "United States Secret Service")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/President_Ronald_Reagan_and_Nancy_Reagan_in_The_Presidential_Limousine_During_The_Inaugural_Parade%2C_Washington%2C_DC_-_DPLA_-_a8b1ab34a866ace74c6f3161eacbf046.jpg/250px-President_Ronald_Reagan_and_Nancy_Reagan_in_The_Presidential_Limousine_During_The_Inaugural_Parade%2C_Washington%2C_DC_-_DPLA_-_a8b1ab34a866ace74c6f3161eacbf046.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Ronald_Reagan_and_Nancy_Reagan_in_The_Presidential_Limousine_During_The_Inaugural_Parade,_Washington,_DC_-_DPLA_-_a8b1ab34a866ace74c6f3161eacbf046.jpg)

President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") waves following [his inauguration](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Ronald_Reagan "First inauguration of Ronald Reagan") as the nation's 40th president on January 20, 1981.

The [U.S. Secret Service](/wiki/United_States_Secret_Service "United States Secret Service") is charged with protecting the president and the [first family](/wiki/First_family_of_the_United_States "First family of the United States"). As part of their protection, presidents, [first ladies](/wiki/First_Lady_of_the_United_States "First Lady of the United States"), their children and other immediate family members, and other prominent persons and locations are assigned [Secret Service codenames](/wiki/Secret_Service_codename "Secret Service codename").[[176]](#cite_note-junior-182) The use of such names was originally for security purposes and dates to a time when sensitive electronic communications were not routinely [encrypted](/wiki/Encryption "Encryption"); today, the names simply serve for purposes of brevity, clarity, and tradition.[[177]](#cite_note-cbs-codenames-183)

## Post-presidency

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Five_Presidents_Oval_Office.jpg/250px-Five_Presidents_Oval_Office.jpg)](/wiki/File:Five_Presidents_Oval_Office.jpg)

From left to right: Presidents [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush"), [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"), [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), and [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") in the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office") on January 7, 2009; Obama took office thirteen days later.

### Activities

Some former presidents have had significant careers after leaving office. Prominent examples include [William Howard Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft "William Howard Taft")'s tenure as [chief justice of the United States](/wiki/Chief_justice_of_the_United_States "Chief justice of the United States") and [Herbert Hoover](/wiki/Herbert_Hoover "Herbert Hoover")'s work on government reorganization after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Two former presidents served in Congress after leaving the White House: [John Quincy Adams](/wiki/John_Quincy_Adams "John Quincy Adams") was elected to the House of Representatives, serving there for 17 years, and [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson") returned to the Senate in 1875, though he died soon after. Two former presidents returned to the presidency: [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland") lost in [1888](/wiki/1888_United_States_presidential_election "1888 United States presidential election") and won in [1892](/wiki/1892_United_States_presidential_election "1892 United States presidential election"), and [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") lost in [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") and won in [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"). Some ex-presidents were very active, especially in international affairs, including Theodore Roosevelt;[[178]](#cite_note-184) Herbert Hoover;[[179]](#cite_note-185) Richard Nixon;[[180]](#cite_note-186) and Jimmy Carter.[[181]](#cite_note-187)[[182]](#cite_note-188)

Earlier presidents returned to their hometowns to lead charitable work there, or supervised farm work on their farms or plantations, many of which had been neglected during their presidencies. [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") founded the [University of Virginia](/wiki/University_of_Virginia "University of Virginia") during his post-presidency, and, following his death, was succeeded as rector by [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"). Madison and [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe "James Monroe") participated in the drafting of one version of the [Virginia Constitution](/wiki/Virginia_Constitution "Virginia Constitution"), while [John Tyler](/wiki/John_Tyler "John Tyler") served at the Confederate Constitution and in the Confederate Congress.

Presidents may use their predecessors as emissaries to deliver private messages to other nations or as official representatives of the United States to state funerals and other important foreign events.[[183]](#cite_note-apreaction19810331-189)[[184]](#cite_note-4presidentsphotos-190) Richard Nixon made multiple foreign trips to countries including China and Russia and was lauded as an elder statesman.[[185]](#cite_note-191) [During his post-presidency](/wiki/Post-presidency_of_Jimmy_Carter "Post-presidency of Jimmy Carter"), [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") became a global [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") campaigner, international arbiter, and election monitor, as well as a recipient of the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize"). Bill Clinton also worked as an informal ambassador, most recently in the negotiations that led to the release of two American [journalists](/wiki/Journalist "Journalist"), [Laura Ling](/wiki/Laura_Ling "Laura Ling") and [Euna Lee](/wiki/Euna_Lee "Euna Lee"), from [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea").

During his presidency, George W. Bush called on former presidents George H. W. Bush and Clinton to assist with humanitarian efforts after the [2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_and_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami"). President Obama followed suit by asking presidents Clinton and George W. Bush to lead efforts to aid Haiti after an [earthquake](/wiki/2010_Haiti_earthquake "2010 Haiti earthquake") devastated that country in 2010. Clinton's relationship with the two Bushes is not the first friendship between former presidents: Carter enjoyed a friendship and correspondence with [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford"), while Jefferson and [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams") had an extended correspondence through letters in their post-presidencies.

[Clinton has been active politically since his presidential term ended](/wiki/Post-presidency_of_Bill_Clinton "Post-presidency of Bill Clinton"), working with his wife [Hillary](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") on her [2008](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton_2008_presidential_campaign "Hillary Clinton 2008 presidential campaign") and [2016](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton_2016_presidential_campaign "Hillary Clinton 2016 presidential campaign") presidential bids and President Obama on his [2012 reelection campaign](/wiki/Barack_Obama_2012_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2012 presidential campaign"). Obama has also been active politically since his presidential term ended, having worked with his former vice president [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") on his [2020 election campaign](/wiki/Joe_Biden_2020_presidential_campaign "Joe Biden 2020 presidential campaign") and Biden's vice president [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") on her [2024 election campaign](/wiki/Kamala_Harris_2024_presidential_campaign "Kamala Harris 2024 presidential campaign") after President Biden [withdrew his bid for re-election](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_Joe_Biden_from_the_2024_United_States_presidential_election "Withdrawal of Joe Biden from the 2024 United States presidential election").

After losing his bid for the presidency in 2020, Trump remained politically active and was an outspoken critic of his successor and the Democratic Party. He also contended with four criminal cases. Trump announced his fourth bid to the presidency in 2022, ultimately becoming the nominee of his party for the third time and won a second presidential term in 2024.

### Pension and other benefits

The [Former Presidents Act](/wiki/Former_Presidents_Act "Former Presidents Act") (FPA), enacted in 1958, grants lifetime benefits to former presidents and their widows, including a monthly pension, medical care in military facilities, health insurance, and Secret Service protection; also provided is funding for a certain number of staff and for office expenses. The act has been amended several times to provide increases in presidential pensions and in the allowances for office staff. The FPA excludes any president who was removed from office by [impeachment](/wiki/Federal_impeachment_in_the_United_States "Federal impeachment in the United States").[[186]](#cite_note-CRS-192)

According to a 2008 report by the [Congressional Research Service](/wiki/Congressional_Research_Service "Congressional Research Service"):[[186]](#cite_note-CRS-192)

> Chief executives leaving office prior to 1958 often entered retirement pursuing various occupations and received no federal assistance. When industrialist Andrew Carnegie announced a plan in 1912 to offer $25,000 annual pensions to former Presidents, many Members of Congress deemed it inappropriate that such a pension would be provided by a private corporation executive. That same year, legislation was first introduced to create presidential pensions, but it was not enacted. In 1955, such legislation was considered by Congress because of former President Harry S. Truman's financial limitations in hiring an office staff

The pension has increased numerous times with congressional approval. Retired presidents receive a pension based on the salary of the current administration's cabinet secretaries, which was $199,700 per year in 2012.[[187]](#cite_note-193) Former presidents who served in Congress may also collect [congressional pensions](/wiki/Congressional_pension "Congressional pension").[[188]](#cite_note-194) The act also provides former presidents with travel funds and [franking](/wiki/Franking "Franking") privileges.

Prior to 1997, all former presidents, their spouses, and their children until age 16 were protected by the Secret Service until the president's death.[[189]](#cite_note-195)[[190]](#cite_note-196) In 1997, Congress passed legislation limiting Secret Service protection to no more than 10 years from the date a president leaves office.[[191]](#cite_note-secretservice.gov-197) On January 10, 2013, President Obama signed legislation reinstating lifetime Secret Service protection for him, [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"), and all subsequent presidents.[[192]](#cite_note-198) A [first spouse](/wiki/First_Spouse_of_the_United_States "First Spouse of the United States") who remarries is no longer eligible for Secret Service protection.[[191]](#cite_note-secretservice.gov-197)

### Writing

Main article: [List of autobiographies by presidents of the United States](/wiki/List_of_autobiographies_by_presidents_of_the_United_States "List of autobiographies by presidents of the United States")

Since the publication of [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant")'s *[Personal Memoirs](/wiki/Personal_Memoirs_of_U._S._Grant "Personal Memoirs of U. S. Grant")* in 1885, most presidents, including every president who survived to the end of their term from Calvin Coolidge to Barack Obama, has written at least one autobiography. These autobiographies serve two purposes: to ensure the First Family's financial security (Clinton made over $30 million from writing two books[[193]](#cite_note-199)), but also to defend or rehabilitate presidential legacies.

### Presidential libraries and historic sites

Main articles: [Presidential library system](/wiki/Presidential_library_system "Presidential library system") and [List of residences of presidents of the United States](/wiki/List_of_residences_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "List of residences of presidents of the United States")

The homes where U.S. presidents were born, lived or vacationed have frequently been preserved as [house museums](/wiki/House_museum "House museum") and listed as [National Historic Sites](/wiki/List_of_national_historic_sites_and_historical_parks_of_the_United_States "List of national historic sites and historical parks of the United States") or on [U.S. National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/U.S._National_Register_of_Historic_Places "U.S. National Register of Historic Places").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/George_W._Bush_Presidential_Center_dedication.tif/lossy-page1-250px-George_W._Bush_Presidential_Center_dedication.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_W._Bush_Presidential_Center_dedication.tif)

From left to right: Presidents [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"), [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush"), and [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") at the dedication of the [George W. Bush Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/George_W._Bush_Presidential_Library_and_Museum "George W. Bush Presidential Library and Museum") in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") in 2013

Every president since [Herbert Hoover](/wiki/Herbert_Hoover "Herbert Hoover") has created a [repository](/wiki/Institutional_repository "Institutional repository") known as a [presidential library](/wiki/Presidential_library_system "Presidential library system") for preserving and making available his papers, records, and other documents and materials. Completed libraries are deeded to and maintained by the [National Archives and Records Administration](/wiki/National_Archives_and_Records_Administration "National Archives and Records Administration") (NARA); the initial funding for building and equipping each library must come from private, non-federal sources.[[194]](#cite_note-200) There are currently thirteen presidential libraries in the NARA system. There are also presidential libraries maintained by state governments and private foundations and Universities of Higher Education, including:

* The [Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln_Presidential_Library_and_Museum "Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library and Museum"), which is run by the State of [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois");
* The [George W. Bush Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/George_W._Bush_Presidential_Center "George W. Bush Presidential Center"), which is run by [Southern Methodist University](/wiki/Southern_Methodist_University "Southern Methodist University");
* The [George H. W. Bush Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/George_H.W._Bush_Presidential_Library_and_Museum "George H.W. Bush Presidential Library and Museum"), which is run by [Texas A&M University](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_University "Texas A&M University"); and
* The [Lyndon Baines Johnson Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_Library_and_Museum "Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum"), which is run by the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin").

Several former presidents have overseen the building and opening of their own presidential libraries. Some even made arrangements for their own burial at the site. Several presidential libraries contain the graves of the president they document:

* The [Harry S. Truman Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman_Presidential_Library_and_Museum "Harry S. Truman Presidential Library and Museum") in [Independence, Missouri](/wiki/Independence,_Missouri "Independence, Missouri");
* The [Dwight D. Eisenhower Presidential Library, Museum and Boyhood Home](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower_Presidential_Library,_Museum_and_Boyhood_Home "Dwight D. Eisenhower Presidential Library, Museum and Boyhood Home") in [Abilene, Kansas](/wiki/Abilene,_Kansas "Abilene, Kansas");
* The [Richard Nixon Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/Richard_Nixon_Presidential_Library_and_Museum "Richard Nixon Presidential Library and Museum") in [Yorba Linda, California](/wiki/Yorba_Linda,_California "Yorba Linda, California"); and
* The [Ronald Reagan Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_Presidential_Library_and_Museum "Ronald Reagan Presidential Library and Museum") in [Simi Valley, California](/wiki/Simi_Valley,_California "Simi Valley, California").

These gravesites are open to the general public. In some cases (such as with Nixon), the presidential library is adjacent to one of the president's former homes, where in other cases, such as with Lyndon Johnson and Jimmy Carter, the presidential library and the historic site are in different cities entirely.

## Political affiliation

[Political parties](/wiki/Political_parties_in_the_United_States "Political parties in the United States") have dominated [American politics](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_States "Politics of the United States") for most of the nation's history. Though the [Founding Fathers](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States") generally spurned political parties as divisive and disruptive, and their rise had not been anticipated when the U.S. Constitution was drafted in 1787, organized political parties developed in the U.S. in the mid-1790s nonetheless. They evolved from [political factions](/wiki/Political_faction "Political faction"), which began to appear almost immediately after the Federal government came into existence. Those who supported the [Washington administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_George_Washington "Presidency of George Washington") were referred to as "pro-administration" and would eventually form the [Federalist Party](/wiki/Federalist_Party "Federalist Party"), while those in opposition largely joined the emerging [Democratic-Republican Party](/wiki/Democratic-Republican_Party "Democratic-Republican Party").[[195]](#cite_note-201)

Greatly concerned about the very real capacity of political parties to destroy the fragile unity holding the nation together, Washington remained [unaffiliated](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") with any political faction or party throughout his eight-year presidency. He was, and remains, the only U.S. president never to be affiliated with a political party.[[196]](#cite_note-GWpps-202)[[197]](#cite_note-203) Since Washington, every U.S. president has been affiliated with a political party at the time of assuming office, and, since 1869, every president has been a member of either the [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") or [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") Parties.[[198]](#cite_note-204)[[199]](#cite_note-205)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-11)** In addition to $100,000 in travel reimbursement, $19,000 for entertainment and $50,000 for additional expenses.[[10]](#cite_note-cnbc-10)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** The informal term POTUS originated in the [Phillips Code](/wiki/Phillips_Code "Phillips Code"), a shorthand method created in 1879 by [Walter P. Phillips](/wiki/Walter_P._Phillips "Walter P. Phillips") for the rapid transmission of press reports by telegraph.[[11]](#cite_note-12)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-22)** The nine vice presidents who succeeded to the presidency upon their predecessor's death or resignation and served for the remainder of his term are: [John Tyler](/wiki/John_Tyler "John Tyler") (1841); [Millard Fillmore](/wiki/Millard_Fillmore "Millard Fillmore") (1850); [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson") (1865); [Chester A. Arthur](/wiki/Chester_A._Arthur "Chester A. Arthur") (1881); [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") (1901); [Calvin Coolidge](/wiki/Calvin_Coolidge "Calvin Coolidge") (1923); [Harry S. Truman](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman "Harry S. Truman") (1945); [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") (1963); and [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford") (1974).
4. **[^](#cite_ref-24)** [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland") served two non-consecutive terms, and [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") is currently serving his second non-consecutive term, so each is counted twice.[[20]](#cite_note-23)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-41)** Nearly all scholars rank Lincoln among the nation's top three presidents, with many placing him first. See [Historical rankings of presidents of the United States](/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "Historical rankings of presidents of the United States") for a collection of survey results.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-66)** See [List of United States presidential elections by popular vote margin](/wiki/List_of_United_States_presidential_elections_by_popular_vote_margin "List of United States presidential elections by popular vote margin").
7. **[^](#cite_ref-206)** Republican Abraham Lincoln was elected for a second term as part of the [National Union Party](/wiki/National_Union_Party_(United_States) "National Union Party (United States)") ticket with Democrat Andrew Johnson in 1864.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-207)** Former Democrat John Tyler was elected vice president on the Whig Party ticket with Harrison in 1840. Tyler's policy priorities as president soon proved to be opposed to most of the Whig agenda, and he was expelled from the party in September 1841.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-208)** Democrat Andrew Johnson was elected vice president on the National Union Party ticket with Republican Abraham Lincoln in 1864. Later, while president, Johnson tried and failed to build a party of loyalists under the National Union banner. Near the end of his presidency, Johnson rejoined the Democratic Party.

Instead, it relies on reporting requirements that, if triggered, begin the clock running for Congress to approve the particular armed conflict.
